title: Twisted topological tangles or: the knot theory of knitting
speaker: Elisabetta Matsumoto
abstract: Imagine a 1D curve, then use it to fill a 2D manifold that covers an arbitrary 3D object – this computationally intensive materials challenge has been realized in the ancient technology known as knitting. This process for making functional materials 2D materials from 1D portable cloth dates back to prehistory, with the oldest known examples dating from the 11th century CE. Knitted textiles are ubiquitous as they are easy and cheap to create, lightweight, portable, flexible and stretchy.
tags: Workshop on Topology: Identifying Order in Complex Systems

proof_stages.label: Knitting as local coded loop topology
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up knitting as a local code of loop-through-loop operations, distinguishing it from weaving and introducing knit/purl stitches, course/wale directions, and basic knit-purl fabric behavior.

proof_stages.label: Periodic stitch model in T^2 x I and S^3
proof_stages.summary: A fundamental translational domain of a stitch is closed as a link in the thickened torus T^2 x I, then represented in S^3 by viewing T^2 x I as the Hopf-link complement so that standard knot tools can be applied.

proof_stages.label: Swatch grammar operations
proof_stages.summary: A swatch is defined using longitudes, contractible loops, fixed boundary portions, allowed Reidemeister moves and planar isotopies, band surgeries, a canonical course/wale framing, and composition by cutting and gluing swatches.

proof_stages.label: Ribbon obstruction for knittability
proof_stages.summary: The topological constraint reported is that every knittable stitch gives a ribbon knot, while the converse fails, with the proof tied to the canonical swatch axes and corresponding Dehn filling choices.

proof_stages.label: Yarn-scale experimental and simulation model
proof_stages.summary: The mechanics part builds stress-strain experiments and a minimal simulation model by decomposing stitches into crossover and connecting regions, imposing yarn-length constraints, and minimizing bending/contact energies using Bezier-curve representations.

proof_stages.label: Elastic response from crossing-change symmetry
proof_stages.summary: Experimental and simulated stress-strain curves are interpreted by tracking how knit-to-purl crossing changes create even or odd connecting segments, yielding stiff bending-dominated modes or soft rotational modes.

proof_stages.label: Constitutive law and defect-driven shape program
proof_stages.summary: The final stage extracts a nonlinear constitutive model from bending-length regimes and proposes extending the topology-mechanics framework to knitted defects such as increases, decreases, and short rows that generate shape.

examples.name: Cow hitch stitch
examples.note: A candidate ribbon-knot configuration led to an actually hand-knittable new stitch, so the example is presented as mathematics producing a new knitting move.
examples.engagement_reason: The cow hitch stitch is presented as a new stitch inspired by a ribbon-knot idea, but its construction or properties are not worked through in detail.

passes.reason: The cow hitch stitch was considered as a possible counterexample but is shown to be knittable and otherwise not a true classification exception.

examples.name: Raw hems and runs in stockings
examples.note: The speaker uses raw-cut American Apparel-style hems and stocking runs to illustrate the difference between generic cuts that are mechanically stable and loop ladders that unravel locally.
examples.engagement_reason: Raw hems and stocking runs are reasoned through mechanically, explaining why cut hems remain stable and why runs unzip as a ladder of loops.

passes.reason: Raw hems and stocking runs are used to test robustness expectations, showing respectively that generic cuts need not unravel and that runs are non-robust.
